claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my child. His name is Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know about Alex!

To help assess your eligibility, could you please tell me how old Alex is?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 17, I need to ask a bit more about his current situation.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels, NVQs up to level 3, or similar), or enrolled on an approved training course?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in college or doing A-levels or anything like that. But he has started an apprenticeship recently.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know.

Could you tell me whether Alex's apprenticeship is paid or unpaid? This is relevant because it can affect eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's a paid apprenticeship - he's training in a trade.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I just need to gather a bit more information to complete the assessment.

Does Alex live with you? And are you responsible for his day-to-day care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Based on what you've told me about Alex, I now have enough information to make an assessment.

Let me summarise what I've learned:
- Alex is 17 years old
- He is on a **paid apprenticeship** in England
- He lives with you

According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you will **stop getting Child Benefit** if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. This applies specifically to paid apprenticeships.

Let me now provide you with the formal eligibility outcome:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Reason:** Alex is 17 years old and has started a paid apprenticeship in England. Under the Child Benefit rules, eligibility stops when a child starts an apprenticeship in England — even if they still live at home with you.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he leaves the apprenticeship and returns to approved education or training), you may become eligible again at that point.

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any questions or if Alex's situation changes, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started a paid apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you will stop getting Child Benefit if your child 'starts an apprenticeship in England'. Since Alex is on an apprenticeship in England, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. This rule applies regardless of whether the child still lives at home or whether the parent is responsible for their care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 17), who has recently started a paid apprenticeship in England. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility ends when a child starts an apprenticeship in England. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started a paid apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you will stop getting Child Benefit if your child 'starts an apprenticeship in England'. Since Alex is on an apprenticeship in England, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. This rule applies regardless of whether the child still lives at home or whether the parent is responsible for their care.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 17), who has recently started a paid apprenticeship in England. Under Child Benefit rules, eligibility ends when a child starts an apprenticeship in England. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex.
